scope.summary: Implement fabric-aware strain map computation that weights the per-vertex strain values by the panel's warp/weft anisotropic compliance, distinguishing directional strain (warp-direction over-stretch vs weft-direction over-stretch) and producing separate warp-strain and weft-strain heatmap channels
acceptance_criteria: AnisotropicStrainMap type stores per-vertex warp_strain and weft_strain f32 arrays in addition to the scalar strain
compute_anisotropic_strain(mesh, material_frame, stretch_lambdas) decomposes strain along each grain axis using the MaterialFrameParams warp/weft directions
The heatmap overlay mode accepts warp_strain and weft_strain as additional mode options alongside stress/strain/pressure
A silk panel (high weft compliance, low warp compliance) produces visibly different warp vs weft strain patterns compared to a denim panel under identical forces
cargo test anisotropic_strain::test_silk_vs_denim_strain_difference passes: max warp_strain for denim panel >= 2x max warp_strain for silk panel under the same applied force
scope.risk_if_missed: Isotropic strain maps mislead the pattern maker: a denim panel and a silk panel with the same scalar strain value require completely different pattern corrections because their stiffness is directional. Without anisotropic decomposition, fit correction guidance is systematically wrong for structured fabrics.

risks: Broad MT scope can hide multiple review targets.
Markdown MT lists may disagree with packet-declared active/next MT truth.
Receipt routing may target an old MT if contract identity is omitted.
minimum_controls: Exactly one primary proof target per MT unless the packet records why not.
MT identity must be stable and referenced by receipt correlation.
Generated projection drift must not wake Coder or Validator by itself.
